revision.comment: Bot: tag page without wikidata link [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23124421395|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Innermost gulf of the Yellow Sea and Korea Bay on the coast of Northeastern and North China}} {{Distinguish|Bohai Bay}} {{Other uses|Bohai (disambiguation)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox Chinese |pic = Bohai Sea map.png |piccap = |c = {{linktext|渤海}} |p = Bó Hǎi |wuu = Poh{{sup|平}} hae{{sup|平}} |w = Po{{sup|2}} hai{{sup|3}} |mi = {{IPAc-cmn|b|o|2|.|h|ai|3}} |ci = |altname = |j = But{{sup|6}} hoi{{sup|2}} |y = Buht hói |tl = Phu̍t hái }} The '''Bohai Sea''' ({{lang-zh|c=渤海|p=Bó Hǎi|l=Bo Sea}}) is a [[gulf]]/[[inland sea]] approximately {{convert|77,000|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} in area on the east coast of [[Mainland China]].<ref>[http://www.gov.cn/guoqing/2017-07/28/content_5043915.htm 中华人民共和国版图] {{inlang|zh}}</ref> It is the northwestern and innermost extension of the [[Yellow Sea]], to which it connects via the Bohai Strait. It has a mean depth of approximately {{convert|18|m|sp=us}}, with a maximum depth of about {{convert|80|m|sp=us}} located in the northern part of the Bohai Strait.<ref name="BohaiSeaSedimentation" />
The Bohai Sea is enclosed by three [[provinces of China|provinces]] and one [[direct-administered municipalities of China|direct-administered municipality]] from three different [[list of regions of China|regions]] of [[China]]—[[Liaoning|Liaoning Province]] (of [[Northeast China]]), [[Hebei|Hebei Province]] and [[Tianjin|Tianjin Municipality]] (of [[North China]]), and [[Shandong|Shandong Province]] (of [[East China]]). It is the center of the [[Bohai Economic Rim]], and its proximity to the Chinese capital of Beijing and the [[direct-administered municipalities of China|municipality]] of [[Tianjin]] makes it one of the busiest seaways in the world. The entrance to the Bohai Sea is considered a part of the [[territorial sea]] of the People's Republic of China due to the presence of the [[Changdao County|Miaodao islands]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=四大海域，为什么只有渤海属于内海，领海内允许他国船只航行吗？_腾讯新闻 |url=https://new.qq.com/rain/a/20220505A02OVB00 |access-date=2023-03-25 |website=new.qq.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Marine Regions |url=https://www.marineregions.org/eezdetails.php?mrgid=8486 |access-date=2023-03-25 |website=www.marineregions.org}}</ref> China declared the Bohai sea to be part of its inland waters in 1958.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Gard Alert: Bohai Sea, China – fines for discharge of pollutants |url=https://www.gard.no/web/articles?documentId=20894781 |access-date=2023-03-25 |website=www.gard.no}}</ref>
==History==
=== Ancient times === During the [[Pleistocene]], the Bohai Sea experienced numerous [[glacio-eustatic|glacioeustatic]] [[marine transgression|transgressions]] and [[marine regression|regressions]], as evidenced by sediment cores sampled from the seafloor showing fluvial floodplain conditions during intervals of low sea level.<ref name="BohaiSeaSedimentation">{{cite journal |last1=Yao |first1=Zhengquan |last2=Shi |first2=Xuefa |last3=Liu |first3=Yanguang |last4=Kandasamy |first4=Selvaraj |last5=Qiao |first5=Shuqing |last6=Li |first6=Xiaoyan |last7=Bai |first7=Yazhi |last8=Zhu |first8=Aimei |date=15 July 2020 |title=Sea-level and climate signatures recorded in orbitally-forced continental margin deposits over the last 1 Myr: New perspectives from the Bohai Sea |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0031018220301814 |journal=[[Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology]] |volume=550 |article-number=109736 |doi=10.1016/j.palaeo.2020.109736 |s2cid=216519749 |access-date=5 November 2022|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
=== Imperial era === Throughout imperial Chinese history, the Bohai Sea held both strategic and economic importance. During the [[Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]], it was a vital maritime link to the [[Bohai Kingdom]] (698–926)—an early medieval state founded by the Mohe people in what is now northeast China and parts of the Korean Peninsula. Trade and diplomatic missions between China, Korea, and Japan often passed through the Bohai Sea, making it part of the broader East Asian maritime network. During the [[Liao dynasty|Liao]] (907–1125) and [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin]] (1115–1234) dynasties, which were established by the Khitan and Jurchen peoples respectively, control over the Bohai region was central to their northern dominance. These dynasties maintained fleets and fortified ports along the coast.
In the Yuan Dynasty (1271–1368), the Bohai Sea gained greater attention as maritime commerce expanded under a more unified empire. Naval bases were established along the coast, and shipping routes were actively developed to support both military logistics and trade. The Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1912) dynasties continued to use the Bohai region for maritime patrols and defense, especially as threats from [[Japanese pirates]] (''wokou'') increased during the Ming era. At the same time, regional ports such as [[Yingkou]] and [[Taku Forts|Dagu]] became important for domestic salt, grain, and fish trade.
=== 19th and 20th centuries === Until the early 20th century, Bo Hai was often called the Gulf of Zhili ({{lang-zh|t=直隸海灣|p=Zhílì Hǎiwān|links=no}}) or Gulf of Beizhili ({{lang-zh|t=北直隸海灣|p=Běizhílì Hǎiwān|labels=no}}). The [[romanization of Chinese|romanization systems]] widely used in the West at the time rendered these names as variations of "Jili", "Chihli", "Pechihli", or "Pe-Chihli".<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.fsmitha.com/h1/ch06.htm|title = Civilization on the North China Plain}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.watchfulistudio.com/state-of-yan.html|title=State of Yan|access-date=24 May 2021|archive-date=24 May 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210524054919/https://www.watchfulistudio.com/state-of-yan.html}}</ref> [[Zhili]] and [[North Zhili]] were historic provinces in an area surrounding Beijing that approximately corresponds to what is now Hebei Province.
In the last decade of 19th century, Bohai Gulf became the major stage of military conflicts. In 1894-95, the [[First Sino-Japanese War]] broke out, and it featured critical naval engagements in and around the Bohai Sea. One of the most decisive battles was the [[Battle of the Yalu River (1894)|Battle of the Yalu River]] in 1894, fought just outside the Bohai Sea's eastern approaches, where the Qing navy's [[Beiyang Fleet]] suffered a major defeat. This loss opened the path for Japanese forces to project power deeper into the Bohai region. Subsequently, in early 1895, Japanese troops invaded the Shandong Peninsula and captured [[Weihaiwei]], a heavily fortified naval base at the eastern mouth of the Bohai Sea. The fall of Weihaiwei marked the collapse of Qing maritime defense and helped force China to sign the humiliating Treaty of Shimonoseki, in which it ceded Taiwan and recognized Korea's independence.
Several years later, during the [[Boxer Rebellion]] (1899–1901), the Bohai Sea again became strategically important. As the anti-foreign Boxer uprising spread across northern China and reached Beijing, the port city of Tianjin, located at the western edge of the Bohai Sea, became a focal point for foreign military intervention. In June 1900, an international alliance of eight powers launched an amphibious landing at the [[Dagu Forts]], which guarded the mouth of the [[Hai River]] and access to Tianjin and Beijing. After a fierce bombardment and ground assault, the Dagu Forts fell to the alliance forces. This opened the way for allied troops to advance inland through Tianjin and eventually reach and occupy Beijing, forcing the Qing court to flee and leading to the signing of the [[Boxer Protocol]] in 1901.
== Geography == [[File:Rocky shore in Dalian.jpg|thumb|Rocky shore in [[Dalian]], Liaoning|alt=]] There are three major bays inside the Bohai Gulf: [[Laizhou Bay]] to the south, [[Bohai Bay]] to the west, and [[Liaodong Bay]] to the north. The [[provinces of China|provincial-level administrative divisions]] that have a coastline to the Bohai Sea are, from the south going clockwise: [[Shandong]], Hebei, [[Tianjin]] (Tientsin), Hebei again, and [[Liaoning]]. Some of the major rivers draining into the gulf include the [[Yellow River]], [[Xiaoqing River]], [[Hai River]], [[Luan River]], [[Dai River (Hebei)|Dai River]], [[Daling River]], Xiaoling River, [[Liao River]] and [[Daliao River]]. There are a few important oil reserves in the vicinity of the gulf, including the [[Shengli Field]]. Important islands or island groups in the gulf include the [[Changshan Islands|Changshan Archipelago]], Juehua Island ({{lang-zh|s=觉华岛|t=覺華島|labels=no}}), [[Bijia Mountain]], [[Changxing Island (Dalian)|Changxing Island]], [[Xizhong Island]], the East/West Mayi Islands ({{lang-zh|s=蚂蚁岛|labels=no}}), Zhu Island ({{lang-zh|s=猪岛|t=豬島|labels=no}}) and She Island ({{lang-zh|s=蛇岛|t=蛇島|labels=no}}).
=== Bohai Strait === The opening of the Bohai Sea is bounded by the Changshan Archipelago between [[Dalian]]'s [[Lüshunkou District]] on the southern tip of [[Liaodong Peninsula]], and the [[cape (geography)|Cape]] of [[Penglai, Shandong|Penglai]] on the northernmost protrusion of [[Shandong Peninsula]]. Due to its proximity to the capital city Beijing and the population of its surrounding provinces exceeding 210 million, the exit of the Bohai Gulf to the [[Yellow Sea]], the Bohai Strait ({{lang-zh|s=渤海海峡|t=渤海海峽|labels=no}}), has become one of busiest sea routes in recent times. Due to the [[Changshan Islands|Changshan Island Chain]] traversing the southern half of the strait, the strait is subdivided into several [[channel (geography)|channel]]s:
(from north to south, the most commonly used ones in '''bold''')
* '''Laotieshan Channel''' ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|老铁山水道}}), also known as the '''Lau-ti-shan Channel''', is the widest and deepest * Daqin Channel ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|大钦水道}}) * Xiaoqin Channel ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|小钦水道}}) * North Tuoji Channel ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|北砣矶水道}}) * South Tuoji Channel ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|南砣矶水道}}) * '''Changshan Channel''' ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|长山水道}}), is the most direct route to [[Tianjin]] * '''Dengzhou Channel''' ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|登州水道}}), also known as the '''Miaodao Channel''' ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|庙岛水道}}) or '''Miaodao Strait''' ({{lang|zh-Hans-CN|庙岛海峡}}), is the nearest to the shore but also the shallowest
==Major ports== [[File:Seaways Plan for the Bohai Sea.svg|thumb|right|364x364px|China MSA's Seaways Plan for the Bohai Sea. Planned routes follow closely the seaways currently in use]]
There are five major ports along the Bohai Sea rim, with throughputs over 100 million tons, though the port of Tangshan is further subdivided into Jingtang and Caofeidian:
*[[Port of Yingkou]] ({{lang-zh|s=营口港|t=營口港|labels=no}}) *[[Qinhuangdao Port]] ({{lang-zh|s=秦皇岛|t=秦皇島港|labels=no}}) *[[Port of Jingtang]] ({{lang-zh|s=京唐港|labels=no}}) *[[Port of Tangshan]] ({{lang-zh|s=唐山港|labels=no}}) *[[Port of Tangshan]] Caofeidian ({{lang-zh|s=曹妃甸港|labels=no}}) *[[Port of Tianjin|Tianjin Port]] ({{lang-zh|s=天津港|labels=no}}) *[[Port of Huanghua]] ({{lang-zh|s=黄骅港|t=黃驊港|labels=no}})
Caofeidian and Jingtang are usually treated as one port for statistical purposes. The ports of [[Port of Dalian|Dalian]] and [[Port of Yantai|Yantai]] are also traditionally considered part of the Bohai rim, even though strictly speaking they lie outside the limits of the sea. The [[Port of Longkou]] reached 70 million tons of cargo in 2013, and is expected to reach the 100 million ton landmark in the near future.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.chinanews.com/gn/2013/12-23/5652663.shtml|title = 丹东港吞吐量突破1亿吨 正式跻身大港俱乐部-中新网}}</ref>
==Major cities along the Bohai Sea coast== * [[Liaoning]] ** [[Dalian]] ** [[Yingkou]] ** [[Panjin]] ** [[Jinzhou]] ** [[Huludao]] * [[Hebei]] ** [[Qinhuangdao]] ** [[Tangshan]] ** [[Cangzhou]] * [[Tianjin municipality|Tianjin]] * [[Shandong]] ** [[Yantai]] *** [[Zhifu]] *** [[Penglai, Yantai|Penglai]] *** [[Longkou]] *** [[Zhaoyuan, Shandong|Zhaoyuan]] *** [[Laizhou]] *** [[Muping]] *** [[Fushan, Yantai|Fushan]] ** [[Weifang]] *** [[Hanting]] ** [[Dongying]] *** [[Kenli]] ** [[Binzhou]] <gallery mode="packed"> File:Beidaihe panorama from the south.jpg|[[Beidaihe, Qinhuangdao|Beidaihe]], [[Qinhuangdao]] File:Yantai2017.jpg|[[Zhifu, Yantai|Zhifu]], [[Yantai]] File:View of Binhai New Area across the Hai River, July 2022.jpg|[[Binhai, Tianjin|Binhai]], [[Tianjin]] File:Lushunkou 01.JPG|[[Lvshun|Lüshun]], [[Dalian]] File:Xingcheng, Huludao, Liaoning, China - panoramio - 露天谷仓.jpg|[[Xingcheng]], [[Huludao]] </gallery>
==Hydrocarbon resources== The Bohai Bay contains significant oil and gas reserves,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Long |last2=Zhang |first2=Jinliang |last3=Li |first3=Yang |last4=Yan |first4=Xue |last5=Zhang |first5=Xuecai |date=20 September 2022 |title=Paleosalinity and lake level fluctuations of the 3rd Member of Paleogene Shahejie Formation, Chezhen Sag, Bohai Bay Basin |url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11707-022-0979-0?error=cookies_not_supported&code=ff219e4c-b336-4c86-91eb-584075b2f426 |journal=Frontiers of Earth Science |volume=16 |issue=4 |pages=949–962 |doi=10.1007/s11707-022-0979-0 |s2cid=252424192 |access-date=24 January 2023|url-access=subscription }}</ref> which provide much of China's offshore production. The main field in the region, named [[Shengli Oil Field|Shengli]], has been exploited since the 1960s. It still produces about half a million barrels a day, but is [[peak oil|declining]].<ref name="autogenerated1">[https://web.archive.org/web/20120113160337/http://205.254.135.24/countries/cab.cfm?fips=CH China. Background]. US Energy Information Administration</ref> Production is dominated by Chinese majors ([[China National Offshore Oil Corporation]] (CNOOC) was mostly created for this region), but foreign companies, including [[ConocoPhillips]]<ref>[https://www.conocophillips.com/operations/asia-pacific-middle-east/ Asia Pacific & Middle East]. [[ConocoPhillips]]</ref> and [[Roc Oil Company]],<ref>[http://www.bairdmaritime.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=1635:roc-oil-begins-bohai-bay-oil-production-&catid=70&Itemid=62 Roc Oil begins Bohai Bay oil production] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120316214509/http://www.bairdmaritime.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=1635:roc-oil-begins-bohai-bay-oil-production-&catid=70&Itemid=62 |date=16 March 2012 }}. Bairdmaritime. 14 May 2009</ref> are present. The Gudao Field, located in the Zhanhua [[sedimentary basin]], was discovered in 1968, based on gravity, magnetic and seismic surveys conducted between 1963 and 1964.<ref name="Sizhong">Sizhong, C., and Ping, W., 1980, Geology of Gudao Oil Field and Surrounding Areas, in Giant Oil and Gas Fields of the Decade: 1968–1978, AAPG Memoir 30, Halbouty, M.T., editor, Tulsa: American Association of Petroleum Geologists, {{ISBN|0891813063}}, Pp. 471–486</ref> The reservoir includes the Guantao ([[Miocene]]) and Minghuazhen ([[Pliocene]]) [[geologic formation]]s within the dome-like [[anticline]].<ref name="Sizhong" /> The Suizhong 36-1 Oil Field was discovered in 1987, and produces from [[Oligocene]] [[fluvial]]-[[river delta|deltaic]] and lacustrine [[sandstone]]s.<ref name="Gustavson">Gustavson, J.B., and Gang, X.S., 1992, The Suizhong 36-1 Oil Field, Bohai Gulf, Offshore China, In Giant Oil and Gas Fields of the Decade, 1978–1988, AAPG Memoir 54, Halbouty, M.T., editor, Tulsa: American Association of Petroleum Geologists, {{ISBN|0891813330}}</ref> Oil spills have been reported frequently in this region: three spills occurred in a two-month timeframe in 2011.<ref>[http://www.offshoreenergytoday.com/china-third-oil-spill-in-bohai-sea-in-less-than-two-months/ China: Third Oil Spill in Bohai Sea in Less than Two Months]. Offshore Energy Today (12 July 2011). Retrieved on 3 August 2011.</ref> In 2024, CNOOC discovered a 100 million ton oilfield in Bohai Sea.<ref>{{Cite web |title=China's CNOOC discovers 100m ton oilfield in Bohai Sea |url=https://asia.nikkei.com/Business/Energy/China-s-CNOOC-discovers-100m-ton-oilfield-in-Bohai-Sea |access-date=2024-03-18 |website=Nikkei Asia |language=en-GB}}</ref>
== Tunnel crossing == {{Main|Bohai Strait tunnel}}
In February 2011, [[China]] announced that it would build a road and [[Bohai Strait Tunnel|rail tunnel across the Bohai Strait]] to connect the [[Liaodong Peninsula|Liaodong]] and [[Shandong Peninsula|Shandong peninsula]]s. When completed, the tunnel would be {{convert|106|km|mi|sp=us}} long, making it twice as long as the channel tunnel connecting England and France.<ref>''[[Asahi Shimbun]]'', "China To Build Undersea Tunnel Crossing Bohai Strait", 18 February 2011.</ref> In July 2013, a modified plan was announced, involving a {{convert|123|km|mi|adj=on|sp=us}}-long tunnel between [[Dalian]], Liaoning and [[Yantai]], Shandong.<ref>{{cite news |url = https://www.reuters.com/article/us-china-tunnel-idUSBRE96A0EK20130711 |title = {{-'}}China plans world's longest sea tunnel at $42 billion -report |work = [[Reuters]] |date = 11 July 2013 |access-date = 30 June 2017 |archive-date = 24 September 2015 |archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20150924182842/http://www.reuters.com/article/2013/07/11/us-china-tunnel-idUSBRE96A0EK20130711 |url-status = live }}</ref> The tunnel was first proposed by officials in Yantai in 1992, but a clear plan remained elusive. In 2019, construction had still not started, and the proposed cost had reached 300 billion yuan ($43 billion USD).<ref>{{Cite web |title=China Mulls Next Super Project: The Longest Undersea Tunnel - Caixin Global |url=https://www.caixinglobal.com/2019-05-22/china-mulls-next-super-project-the-longest-undersea-tunnel-101418352.html |access-date=2024-08-23 |website=www.caixinglobal.com |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Balhae]] * [[Bohai Commandery]] * [[Bohai Economic Rim]] * [[Eight Immortals]]
